text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
SURVEILLANCE STUDY — ONCOLOGIC FOLLOW-UP

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Maria Elena Reyes is a 45-year-old woman with a history of left breast stage IIB (ypT1cN1M0 post-neoadjuvant) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3. She underwent neoadjuvant therapy with pembrolizumab, dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide, and weekly paclitaxel, followed by left partial mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection demonstrating residual invasive carcinoma with 3 of 16 lymph nodes positive (RCB-II, non-pCR). She subsequently completed adjuvant locoregional radiation to the left breast and regional nodal basins and is currently on adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab maintenance. This study is obtained for routine oncologic surveillance approximately 6 months following surgery.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with standard oncologic protocol. Helical acquisition was obtained following intravenous administration of 100 mL of iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350) at a rate of 3 mL/sec, with portal venous phase imaging of the abdomen and pelvis (70-second delay) and standard chest acquisition. Multiplanar reformats in coronal and sagittal planes were reviewed. Bone windows examined. No prior imaging available for direct side-by-side comparison on this system; report informed by clinical history and prior imaging documented in the medical record.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The lungs are well-inflated bilaterally. No discrete pulmonary nodule is identified. No suspicious focal consolidation or mass lesion. In the left lung base, there is a minor region of ground-glass opacity with subtle associated mild reticular change, predominantly subpleural in distribution, measuring approximately 2.5 cm in greatest extent. This is consistent with evolving post-radiation inflammatory change and appears to be in the expected radiation field corresponding to the left internal mammary and inferior breast radiation portal. There is no associated volume loss, bronchiectasis, or pleural reaction to suggest organizing pneumonia of greater clinical significance. The right lung is clear throughout. The central airways are patent. No endobronchial lesion. No air-space consolidation to suggest superimposed infection.

Pleurae:
Bilateral pleural spaces are clear. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber throughout. The mediastinum is not widened. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal mass. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Prevascular, precarinal, paratracheal, and subcarinal nodal stations: all lymph nodes are within normal size limits, with the largest subcarinal node measuring approximately 6 mm in short axis. The hilar structures are symmetric and unremarkable. No hilar adenopathy.

Breast and Chest Wall:
The left breast demonstrates expected post-operative and post-radiation changes, with mild diffuse skin thickening and trabecular coarsening of the left breast parenchyma consistent with treatment effect. A small metallic surgical clip is seen in the left breast at the expected lumpectomy bed site, serving as the radiation boost reference marker. No new soft tissue mass is identified in the left breast on this non-dedicated CT evaluation. Right breast: no discrete mass or suspicious density identified. The chest wall musculature is intact bilaterally without evidence of involvement or infiltration.

Axillae:
Left axillary surgical bed: expected post-surgical changes are present, including a small amount of linear soft tissue stranding and minor seroma-like fluid density consistent with the prior left axillary lymph node dissection. No new discrete lymph node enlargement in the left axilla. No left infraclavicular or left supraclavicular adenopathy. Right axillary region: no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes identified. Right-sided nodal stations are unremarkable.

Bones (Chest):
The visualized osseous structures of the thorax, including the thoracic spine, ribs, sternum, and clavicles, appear intact. No lytic or blastic lesion is identified. No cortical destruction or pathologic-appearing fracture. Minor degenerative changes of the lower thoracic spine are incidentally noted.

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and contour. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal hypodense or hyperdense lesion. No hepatic mass, cyst (beyond trace subcentimeter focus at segment VI, too small to characterize but with features suggesting a simple cyst), or suspicious nodule is seen. The portal and hepatic venous structures enhance normally. No biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is present with no wall thickening, gallstones, or pericholecystic fluid. The bile ducts are not dilated.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size, measuring approximately 9.8 cm in craniocaudal dimension. Homogeneous splenic enhancement without focal lesion. No splenic laceration or perisplenic fluid.

Pancreas:
The pancreas enhances uniformly through the head, neck, body, and tail without focal mass, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic stranding. The pancreatic duct is not dilated.

Adrenal Glands:
Both adrenal glands are normal in morphology. No adrenal mass, nodule, or thickening. No adrenal lesion suspicious for metastasis.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric cortical enhancement without focal parenchymal lesion. No renal mass or hydronephrosis. No perinephric stranding. No urinary tract calculus identified on non-delayed images. The ureters appear unremarkable.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel loops appear normal in caliber without wall thickening, mesenteric stranding, or obstructive pattern. No free intraperitoneal air. No bowel mass identified.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
The peritoneal surfaces appear smooth and clean. No peritoneal nodule, omental thickening, or nodularity to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes:
No retroperitoneal or para-aortic adenopathy. The largest visualized retroperitoneal node measures approximately 7 mm in short axis, within normal limits. No mesenteric or portocaval lymph node enlargement. The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal change.

Bones (Abdomen):
The visualized lumbar spine and pelvic bones are intact. No lytic or blastic osseous lesion is identified. No pathologic fracture. Mild facet degenerative change at L4-L5.

PELVIS:

Gynecologic Organs:
The uterus is normal in size and morphology for a premenopausal woman. No uterine or adnexal mass. Bilateral ovaries appear grossly normal. No free pelvic fluid.

Urinary Bladder:
The urinary bladder is partially filled and appears unremarkable. No wall thickening or intraluminal mass.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
No enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes. Common iliac, external iliac, and obturator nodal stations are within normal size limits.

Bones (Pelvis):
The pelvic bones, sacrum, and proximal femora are intact. No lytic or blastic lesion. No cortical irregularity or periosteal reaction suspicious for osseous metastatic involvement.

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of distant metastatic disease in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

2. Minor ground-glass opacity at the left lung base, subpleural and within the expected left breast/internal mammary radiation field — morphology and distribution are most consistent with resolving post-radiation inflammatory change (radiation pneumonitis sequela). No features to suggest progression or new infectious consolidation. Clinical correlation with pulmonary symptoms recommended.

3. Expected post-operative and post-radiation changes in the left breast and left axillary surgical bed, without new soft tissue mass or recurrent adenopathy.

4. No suspicious hepatic, adrenal, splenic, or renal lesion. Tiny subcentimeter hypoattenuating focus in hepatic segment VI, likely a simple cyst, below threshold for further characterization.

5. No peritoneal carcinomatosis. No retroperitoneal, pelvic, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

6. No osseous lesion suspicious for metastatic involvement throughout the entire axial and appendicular skeleton visualized.

Overall, these findings are reassuring and consistent with no evidence of active locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease at this surveillance timepoint. Post-treatment changes are evolving in the expected fashion.
